edge: The problem of modeling data distribution without relying on human-annotated labels is known as unconditional generation. It is a long-standing and fundamental challenge in generative models.
The generation quality of an unconditional method has been worse than that of its conditional counterpart, which can be attributed to the lack of semantic information provided by labels.

Representation-Conditioned Generation (RCG) is a framework that provides an effective solution to the unconditional generation problem without using labels. It generates semantic representations in the representation space produced by a self-supervised encoder.
The semantic representations generated by Representation-Conditioned Generation (RCG) can be used to condition the image generator, closing the gap between unconditional and conditional generation.
RCG significantly improves unconditional generation quality by achieving a new state-of-the-art FID of 2.15 on ImageNet 256×256, reducing the previous best by a relative 64%.

RCG is an unconditional generation solution that largely reduces the reliance of current generative models on manual labels, achieving an unprecedented 2.15 FID for unconditional generation on ImageNet 256×256 benchmark.
RCG massively improves the unconditional generation quality of various image generators such as LDM, ADM, DiT, or MAGE regardless of their specific form.
RCG is a solution that aims to improve generative models by accurately modeling data distribution for unconditional generation, achieving outstanding results on ImageNet 256×256 benchmark.

The diffusion model is developed for unconditional representation generation, known as a representation diffusion model (RDM). RDM employs a fully-connected network with multiple fully-connected residual blocks as its backbone.
RDM follows DDIM for training and inference in representation generation.

During inference, RDM generates representations from Gaussian noise following the DDIM sampling process. RDM operates on highly compacted representations, providing substantial semantic information for image generation.
The image generator in RCG crafts images conditioned on self-supervised representations, providing substantial semantic information for the image generator.
Conceptually, such an image generator can be any modern conditional image generative model by substituting its original conditioning with representations.

RCG achieves outstanding unconditional generation performance with less training cost compared to current generative models.
Specifically, MAGE-B with RCG achieves an unconditional generation FID of 4.87 in less than a day when trained on 64 V100 GPUs.
RCG-G with MAGE-L achieves a FID of 2.15, showing significant improvement in unconditional image generation.
RCG demonstrates a FID of 4.87 in less than a day when trained on 64 V100 GPUs. RCG largely improves the state-of-the-art in unconditional image generation.
In Table 2, a comparison between MAGE with RCG and previous state-of-the-art methods in unconditional image generation is shown.
RCG can generate images with both high fidelity and diversity, achieving an FID of 3.44 and an Inception Score of 186.9.
The guided version of RCG (RCG-G) reaches an FID of 2.15 and an Inception Score of 253.4, significantly surpassing previous methods of unconditional image generation.
RCG’s unconditional generation performance rivals leading methods in class-conditional image generation.

MAGE-L with RCG is comparable to leading class-conditional methods, with and without guidance, showing that RCG improves the performance of unconditional image generation to the same level as state-of-the-art class-conditional generation methods.
